denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has completed its medical necessity review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on July 20, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated July 22, 2026.

After review under the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin, we are unable to approve the requested service. Our medical policy requires documented failure of prerequisite therapies and lower-cost diagnostic options before the requested imaging is considered medically necessary. The submitted records do not establish that the required prerequisite options have been completed.

The appropriateness criteria applied to this determination are characterized in the ages 12 to 17 population. On that basis the requested service does not meet the applicable criteria in effect.

This determination applies the same standard criteria to all requests of this type and does not reflect any review of the member's diagnosis or the individual clinical records submitted with the request.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an internal appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization. Appeals may be submitted in writing to Aetna Appeals, P.O. Box 14463, Lexington, KY 40512, or by calling the number on your ID card.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Medical Review
clinical_context: The patient is a 32-year-old man with about a year of inflammatory back pain: morning stiffness lasting over an hour, buttock pain that alternates sides, and relief with exercise rather than rest. He is HLA-B27 positive with a raised CRP, and his rheumatologist suspects axial spondyloarthritis with sacroiliitis. Conservative management has failed; two NSAIDs at full anti-inflammatory doses, naproxen and then celecoxib, over more than three months gave no durable relief. Plain radiographs of the sacroiliac joints are normal, which is expected early in this disease because radiographic change lags years behind active inflammation. His rheumatologist ordered MRI of the sacroiliac joints without contrast to detect active sacroiliitis, the imaging step recommended for adults under the ASAS axial spondyloarthritis criteria when radiographs are non-diagnostic. Adult ASAS imaging criteria, not a pediatric or adolescent appropriateness standard, govern a 32-year-old. The submitted records document the NSAID failure, the normal radiographs, the HLA-B27 result, and the inflammatory-pain pattern.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Aetna gating SI-joint MRI behind conservative-therapy documentation is realistic; applying ages-12-17 criteria to an adult and case-blind boilerplate are believable algorithmic misfires with clean rebuttals.

denial_logic.reasoning: Vague step-therapy logic citing an unnamed CPB with no specified prerequisites, plus admitted no individualized review; authentically shoddy, fits step_therapy_missing.

clinical_realism.reasoning: Textbook inflammatory back pain, HLA-B27, raised CRP, NSAID failure (naproxen then celecoxib), radiograph-lag rationale, adult ASAS MRI indication.

date_sanity.reasoning: Received Jul 20 / determined Jul 22 2026 in correct order; 180-day window standard; ~1-year symptom course and 3-month NSAID trials stack plausibly.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Generic Aetna CPB / 'our medical policy' umbrella references with no fabricated codes/edition; realistically vague.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Wrong (ages 12-17) appropriateness criteria applied to a 32-year-old adult, admitted no individualized review, and a factually false step-therapy premise (NSAIDs failed, radiographs already normal). Easy appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Wrong appropriateness criteria applied: the determination uses an 'ages 12 to 17 population' standard for a 32-year-old adult, when adult ASAS axial spondyloarthritis imaging criteria govern

Denial admits it applies the same criteria to all requests and performed no review of the member's diagnosis or records, despite documentation of NSAID failure, normal radiographs, and HLA-B27 positivity
Step-therapy premise is factually contradicted: two full-dose NSAIDs over three months failed and SI-joint radiographs were already obtained and normal as expected early in disease
strong_defenses: The plan can argue its step-therapy framework — requiring documented failure of prerequisite therapies and lower-cost diagnostics before advanced imaging — is a facially valid medical-necessity policy, shifting the dispute to whether documentation was adequately submitted
